instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# API Versioning Strategy

Date: 2025-09-05
Status: Accepted

We need to evolve our REST API without breaking existing clients. Mobile apps in particular can't be force-updated.

We chose URL-based versioning with /v1/, /v2/ prefixes. Each major version gets its own router group. Breaking changes increment the major version. Non-breaking additions are added to the current version. Deprecated versions get a 12-month sunset period with Sunset header.

Rate limits are configured per API version and per authentication tier. Free tier gets 60 req/min, Pro gets 600 req/min, Enterprise gets 6000 req/min. Rate limiting uses a sliding window counter in Redis. Each API key maps to a tier via the api_keys table. Rate limit config lives in config/rate_limits.yaml. Middleware checks limits before routing to handlers.

Consequences: URL versioning means old versions have dedicated handler code with some duplication. Rate limit Redis adds an infrastructure dependency. Need monitoring dashboards for rate limit hit rates per tier.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
API Versioning Approach

Our REST API uses URL-based versioning. We prefix all routes with /v1/, /v2/ etc. When we introduce breaking changes, we bump the major version. Additive changes like new fields or endpoints are fine within the same version.

Old API versions are deprecated with a 12-month sunset window. We set the Sunset HTTP header so clients know when to migrate. Rate limiting is enforced per version per tier: Free (60/min), Pro (600/min), Enterprise (6000/min). The sliding window counter lives in Redis and is checked by middleware before handlers execute. API keys map to tiers through the api_keys database table.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use URL-Based Versioning"},{"type":"entity","name":"REST API"},{"type":"entity","name":"Redis"},{"type":"concept","name":"Rate Limiting"},{"type":"concept","name":"API Versioning"},{"type":"concept","name":"Sunset Header"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use URL-Based Versioning","target":"REST API","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use URL-Based Versioning","target":"API Versioning","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Rate Limiting","target":"Redis","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Rate Limiting","target":"REST API","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Use URL-Based Versioning","target":"Sunset Header","relation":"affects"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
Rate Limiting Architecture

We enforce rate limits on our REST API using a sliding window counter stored in Redis. Limits differ by authentication tier: Free (60 requests per minute with burst of 10), Pro (600/min burst 50), Enterprise (6000/min burst 200). The response includes X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset headers. Exceeding the limit returns 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header.

The configuration is stored in config/rate_limits.yaml. API keys map to tiers through the api_keys table. This integrates with our URL-based versioning where each version prefix (/v1/, /v2/) has its own router group and rate limit configuration.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Authentication Strategy Decision

Date: 2025-11-15
Status: Accepted

We need a stateless authentication mechanism for the API that supports both web clients and mobile apps. Requirements: stateless with no server-side session storage, token refresh without re-login, microservice compatibility, and short-lived access tokens.

We chose JWT (JSON Web Tokens) with a dual-token approach. Access tokens are short-lived (15 minutes) and contain user claims. Refresh tokens are long-lived (7 days), stored in httpOnly cookies, and rotated on use.

We picked JWT over session-based auth because mobile clients can't easily manage cookies, microservices need independent token validation, and JWT allows embedding role and permission claims directly. We support OAuth2 for social login but issue our own JWTs afterward.

Token structure includes sub (user_id), workspace, role, features array, exp, and iat fields. Refresh token revocation requires a small deny-list in Redis. All services share the JWT signing key via secrets manager.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use JWT Authentication"},{"type":"decision","name":"Dual-Token Approach"},{"type":"entity","name":"JWT"},{"type":"entity","name":"OAuth2"},{"type":"entity","name":"Redis"},{"type":"concept","name":"Token Rotation"},{"type":"concept","name":"Stateless Auth"},{"type":"concept","name":"Microservice Architecture"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use JWT Authentication","target":"JWT","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use JWT Authentication","target":"Stateless Auth","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Dual-Token Approach","target":"Token Rotation","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Dual-Token Approach","target":"Redis","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use JWT Authentication","target":"Microservice Architecture","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use JWT Authentication","target":"OAuth2","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
JWT Auth Design

Our API authentication uses JSON Web Tokens with dual tokens: a short-lived access token (15 min TTL) carrying user claims like workspace ID, role, and feature flags, and a long-lived refresh token (7 day TTL) stored as an httpOnly cookie that rotates on each use.

We evaluated session-based auth but rejected it. Mobile clients struggle with cookie management, and microservices need to validate tokens independently without hitting a shared session store. OAuth2 is supported for social login flows but we mint our own JWTs post-authentication.

Revocation works through a Redis deny-list for refresh tokens. JWT signing keys are distributed through a secrets manager so all services can validate independently. Clock skew is handled with a 5-second grace period on token expiry checks.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use JWT Authentication"},{"type":"decision","name":"Dual-Token Approach"},{"type":"entity","name":"JWT"},{"type":"entity","name":"Redis"},{"type":"entity","name":"OAuth2"},{"type":"concept","name":"Token Rotation"},{"type":"concept","name":"Stateless Auth"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use JWT Authentication","target":"JWT","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use JWT Authentication","target":"Stateless Auth","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Dual-Token Approach","target":"Token Rotation","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Dual-Token Approach","target":"Redis","relation":"uses"},{"source":"JWT","target":"OAuth2","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
Token Security Notes

Access tokens use JWT with 15-minute expiry. They carry claims: user ID, workspace, role, feature flags. Refresh tokens last 7 days and are stored in httpOnly cookies to prevent XSS theft. On each refresh, the old token is rotated out and added to a Redis deny-list.

All microservices validate JWTs independently using a shared signing key distributed through our secrets manager. We chose this over centralized session validation to avoid a single point of failure and to keep auth stateless. OAuth2 social login is supported but results in our own JWT being issued. There is a 5-second clock skew tolerance on expiry validation.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use JWT Authentication"},{"type":"decision","name":"Rotate Refresh Tokens"},{"type":"entity","name":"JWT"},{"type":"entity","name":"Redis"},{"type":"entity","name":"OAuth2"},{"type":"concept","name":"Token Rotation"},{"type":"concept","name":"Stateless Auth"},{"type":"concept","name":"XSS Prevention"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use JWT Authentication","target":"JWT","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use JWT Authentication","target":"Stateless Auth","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Rotate Refresh Tokens","target":"Redis","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Rotate Refresh Tokens","target":"Token Rotation","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Rotate Refresh Tokens","target":"XSS Prevention","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"JWT","target":"OAuth2","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Database Migration Strategy

Date: 2025-10-20
Status: Accepted

Our PostgreSQL schema needs to evolve safely as we add features. We've had incidents where manual ALTER TABLE statements broke production.

We adopted golang-migrate for all schema changes. All migrations are numbered SQL files in the migrations/ directory. They run automatically on deploy via an init container. Every migration must have an UP and DOWN file. No migration may take a lock longer than 5 seconds, so we use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

Naming convention: NNNN_description.up.sql and NNNN_description.down.sql. Rules: never modify an applied migration, add new columns as nullable then backfill then add NOT NULL, foreign keys use ON DELETE SET NULL unless CASCADE is needed, and every migration gets DBA review.

Current schema is at version 8 with key tables: users, workspaces, api_keys, and audit_log.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use golang-migrate"},{"type":"decision","name":"Auto-Run on Deploy"},{"type":"entity","name":"PostgreSQL"},{"type":"entity","name":"golang-migrate"},{"type":"concept","name":"Schema Migration"},{"type":"concept","name":"Zero-Downtime Deploy"},{"type":"concept","name":"Audit Trail"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use golang-migrate","target":"golang-migrate","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use golang-migrate","target":"PostgreSQL","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use golang-migrate","target":"Schema Migration","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Auto-Run on Deploy","target":"Zero-Downtime Deploy","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Auto-Run on Deploy","target":"golang-migrate","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Schema Migration","target":"Audit Trail","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
DB Migration Approach

We use golang-migrate to manage PostgreSQL schema evolution. Migrations live as numbered SQL files (NNNN_description.up.sql / .down.sql) in the migrations/ folder. They execute automatically during deploys through an init container, so there is no manual SQL in production.

Key rules: no lock held longer than 5 seconds (prefer CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY), never modify an already-applied migration, new columns start nullable then get backfilled before adding NOT NULL. Foreign keys default to ON DELETE SET NULL.

The current schema is version 8. Core tables include users, workspaces (multi-tenant isolation), api_keys (scoped permissions), and audit_log (immutable append-only trail). Rollback is manual via DOWN migrations. Schema version is tracked in the schema_migrations table.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
Schema Safety Practices

PostgreSQL schema changes are managed via golang-migrate. Every change is a pair of numbered SQL files (up and down). Deployments run migrations automatically through an init container before the application starts.

We enforce strict rules: no migration holds a lock longer than 5 seconds, indexes are created concurrently, new columns are always nullable first, and foreign keys default to SET NULL on delete. These rules exist because we previously broke production with manual ALTER TABLE statements.

Schema version 8 includes: users table for accounts, workspaces for tenant isolation, api_keys with scoped permissions per workspace, and an immutable audit_log. Every migration is reviewed by someone with DBA experience before merge.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Error Handling Convention

Date: 2025-07-30
Status: Accepted

Inconsistent error handling across services makes debugging difficult. Some services return raw errors, others swallow them.

All API errors use a structured JSON response with error.code, error.message, and error.request_id. Standard codes: VALIDATION_ERROR (400), UNAUTHORIZED (401), FORBIDDEN (403), RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND (404), RATE_LIMITED (429), INTERNAL_ERROR (500).

Logging: all 5xx errors get full stack traces, 4xx are logged at INFO. We use slog with JSON output. Every log line includes request_id, user_id, workspace_id. Go errors are wrapped with fmt.Errorf and %w. We define sentinel errors: ErrNotFound, ErrAlreadyExists, ErrForbidden.

Consequences: consistent client-side handling, request IDs enable end-to-end tracing, structured logging integrates with Grafana Loki.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Structured Error Responses"},{"type":"decision","name":"Use slog for Logging"},{"type":"entity","name":"slog"},{"type":"entity","name":"Grafana Loki"},{"type":"entity","name":"Go"},{"type":"concept","name":"Error Handling"},{"type":"concept","name":"Observability"},{"type":"concept","name":"Request Tracing"}],"edges":[{"source":"Structured Error Responses","target":"Error Handling","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use slog for Logging","target":"slog","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use slog for Logging","target":"Observability","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use slog for Logging","target":"Grafana Loki","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Structured Error Responses","target":"Request Tracing","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Error Handling","target":"Go","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
Error Handling Standards

Our services use a standardized error format. Every error response is JSON with three fields: code (like VALIDATION_ERROR or INTERNAL_ERROR), message (human-readable), and request_id (for tracing). HTTP status codes map directly: 400 for validation, 401 for auth, 403 for permissions, 404 for missing resources, 429 for rate limits, 500 for server errors.

We log with Go's slog package in structured JSON. 5xx errors include stack traces and full request context. 4xx errors log at INFO level without stack traces. Every log line carries request_id, user_id, and workspace_id for correlation.

In Go code, errors are wrapped using fmt.Errorf with %w to preserve the original. Domain-level sentinel errors (ErrNotFound, ErrAlreadyExists, ErrForbidden) provide type-safe error checking. Logs aggregate into Grafana Loki for analysis.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Structured Error Responses"},{"type":"decision","name":"Use slog for Logging"},{"type":"entity","name":"slog"},{"type":"entity","name":"Grafana Loki"},{"type":"entity","name":"Go"},{"type":"concept","name":"Error Handling"},{"type":"concept","name":"Observability"},{"type":"concept","name":"Sentinel Errors"}],"edges":[{"source":"Structured Error Responses","target":"Error Handling","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use slog for Logging","target":"slog","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use slog for Logging","target":"Grafana Loki","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use slog for Logging","target":"Observability","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Structured Error Responses","target":"Sentinel Errors","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Error Handling","target":"Go","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
Observability and Error Tracing

We use structured logging via Go's slog package with JSON output. Every log entry carries a request_id, user_id, and workspace_id, enabling full request tracing from client to database.

Server errors (5xx) are logged with complete stack traces. Client errors (4xx) are logged at INFO level only. Logs flow into Grafana Loki for aggregation and alerting.

The API itself returns structured JSON errors with a code field (VALIDATION_ERROR, UNAUTHORIZED, FORBIDDEN, RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND, RATE_LIMITED, INTERNAL_ERROR), a human-readable message, and the request_id that ties the response to its server-side log. Go code uses fmt.Errorf with %w for error wrapping and defines sentinel errors (ErrNotFound, ErrAlreadyExists, ErrForbidden) for domain logic.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use slog for Logging"},{"type":"decision","name":"Structured Error Responses"},{"type":"entity","name":"slog"},{"type":"entity","name":"Grafana Loki"},{"type":"entity","name":"Go"},{"type":"concept","name":"Request Tracing"},{"type":"concept","name":"Observability"},{"type":"concept","name":"Sentinel Errors"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use slog for Logging","target":"slog","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use slog for Logging","target":"Grafana Loki","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use slog for Logging","target":"Observability","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Structured Error Responses","target":"Request Tracing","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Request Tracing","target":"Observability","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Structured Error Responses","target":"Go","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Testing Strategy

Date: 2025-08-12
Status: Accepted

We need consistent testing practices across the team. Current coverage is spotty and CI is unreliable.

We follow a test pyramid: unit tests (70%) are fast and isolated with mocked dependencies, integration tests (20%) use real database, real Redis, real HTTP, and E2E tests (10%) use Playwright for critical user flows only.

CI pipeline: lint -> unit tests -> integration tests -> build -> deploy staging -> e2e tests. All PRs must pass lint and unit tests. Integration tests run in parallel using testcontainers. E2E tests run nightly and before production deploys. Coverage threshold: 80% for new packages, 60% for existing.

Conventions: test files are *_test.go in the same package, helpers live in testutil/, table-driven tests for 3+ cases, no time.Sleep in tests. Coverage reports go to Codecov on every PR.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Adopt Test Pyramid"},{"type":"decision","name":"Use testcontainers"},{"type":"entity","name":"Playwright"},{"type":"entity","name":"testcontainers"},{"type":"entity","name":"Codecov"},{"type":"entity","name":"GitHub Actions"},{"type":"entity","name":"Redis"},{"type":"concept","name":"Test Pyramid"},{"type":"concept","name":"CI Pipeline"},{"type":"concept","name":"Code Coverage"}],"edges":[{"source":"Adopt Test Pyramid","target":"Test Pyramid","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use testcontainers","target":"testcontainers","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Adopt Test Pyramid","target":"Playwright","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Adopt Test Pyramid","target":"CI Pipeline","relation":"affects"},{"source":"CI Pipeline","target":"GitHub Actions","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Code Coverage","target":"Codecov","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use testcontainers","target":"Redis","relation":"uses"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
Testing Infrastructure

Our CI pipeline runs in this order: lint, unit tests, integration tests, build, deploy to staging, then E2E tests. We use GitHub Actions as our CI platform. Every pull request must pass linting and unit tests before merge is allowed.

Integration tests use testcontainers to spin up real PostgreSQL and Redis instances in parallel. This avoids flaky mocks while keeping tests isolated. E2E tests use Playwright and run only nightly or before production deploys to keep CI fast at around 8 minutes.

Coverage thresholds are enforced: 80% for new packages, 60% minimum for existing code. Reports upload to Codecov automatically on every PR. Test files follow Go convention: *_test.go colocated with the code, shared helpers in a testutil/ package, and table-driven tests for anything with more than 3 cases.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use testcontainers"},{"type":"decision","name":"Enforce Coverage Thresholds"},{"type":"entity","name":"GitHub Actions"},{"type":"entity","name":"testcontainers"},{"type":"entity","name":"Playwright"},{"type":"entity","name":"Codecov"},{"type":"entity","name":"PostgreSQL"},{"type":"entity","name":"Redis"},{"type":"concept","name":"CI Pipeline"},{"type":"concept","name":"Code Coverage"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use testcontainers","target":"testcontainers","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use testcontainers","target":"PostgreSQL","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use testcontainers","target":"Redis","relation":"uses"},{"source":"CI Pipeline","target":"GitHub Actions","relation":"uses"},{"source":"CI Pipeline","target":"Playwright","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Enforce Coverage Thresholds","target":"Codecov","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Enforce Coverage Thresholds","target":"Code Coverage","relation":"affects"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
E2E and Integration Testing

We split our test suite into three layers following the test pyramid. Unit tests cover 70% and mock all external dependencies. Integration tests cover 20% and use testcontainers to run real PostgreSQL and Redis instances. E2E tests cover 10% and drive Playwright against critical user flows.

The CI pipeline on GitHub Actions sequences these: lint first, then unit tests, then integration tests in parallel, then build, then staging deploy, and finally E2E. Only lint and unit tests are required to merge a PR. E2E runs nightly and pre-deploy.

We chose testcontainers over docker-compose because it integrates directly with Go test lifecycle and provides per-test isolation. Coverage reports are sent to Codecov and must meet thresholds: 80% for new code, 60% for legacy. No time.Sleep in tests is allowed; we use channels or polling with timeouts instead.
